text: A Veress needle is inserted into the umbilicus, and pneumoperitoneum is established. A 10mm trocar is introduced below the xiphoid process, positioned to the left of the midline.

Under direct visualization, the surgeon places five additional trocars: five and twelve millimeter trocars in the left upper quadrant, a five millimeter port in the subxiphoid space, and five and twelve millimeter trocars in the right upper quadrant.

During exploration, the surgeon observes a retrosternal diaphragmatic defect with herniation of the entire small bowel, omentum, and transverse colon, which appears incarcerated and cannot be reduced.
The surgeon begins dissection on the right side of the diaphragmatic defect using blunt traction, dividing adhesions and removing the falciform ligament with traction and ligature.
The surgeon mobilizes the anterior and posterior portions of the hernia sac, achieving complete reduction of the herniated small bowel, omentum, and transverse colon.
The surgeon measures the retrosternal diaphragmatic defect, which is approximately ten by six centimeters in size.

A Jackson Pratt drain is placed in the right lower thoracic cavity and exits through the five millimeter subxiphoid port. The tissue surrounding the hernia defect is cleaned of debris in preparation for mesh placement.
A 10 cm by 15 cm polyester Parietex composite mesh is sutured circumferentially with number one Prolene to repair the diaphragmatic defect.
